TECHNICAL ANALYSIS

Basic Knowledge

Importance of Candle Closures

Welcome | Psychology | Technical analysis | Fundamentals | Traditional Finance | Personal Growth | Insights | Faq | Tools

💡

1. Candle Closures Relevance in a 24/7 Market

The market is made of trading sessions where both bearish and bullish forces battle against each others to push the price down or up, respectively. This “battle” makes the price spiking toward specific areas (usually where there is liquidity) and this is clearly visible by the wicks that a trading session produces. But wicks, a said multiple times, are manipulative tricks to sweep liquidity, they’re not representative of what is truly going on with the “battle” and who’s winning. To do so, we need to look for the body of a candle because that’s where the real trading activity is contained... that’s where the battle is actually ongoing.

Since closures are a summaries of the trading session, you can see closures as testaments of which side has won the battle and is going to continue to do so unless the other faction doesn't show strength, they are like victories over a strategic position in a war. That’s where real smart money intentions are.

  • The bigger the body of the candle, the more strength a faction has.
  • The higher the timeframe of the closure, the more validation it has, thus the more “in control” a side of the market is, especially if the closure occurs in specific regions.

Closures above/below significant levels are a start, but one of the biggest mistakes is not considering the highs or lows of them to assess powerful closures.

When a closure overcomes a SH/SL on HTFs it means that the bull or bear forces have completely defeated their counterpart, therefore they can continue to progress in order to conquer more “strategic positions” and eventually win the war. One important thing to consider is that every time there’s a successful closure, a specific amount of “soldiers” lose the “life” and that's why, at some point, the opposite force will take control reversing the destiny’s battle. An asset can’t go up nor it can go down forever exactly for this reason.

That's why closures are so important in trading.

They tell you who’s winning the battle in that specific timeframe so you can act accordingly depending on your strategy.

Will you ever fight a war with water guns while the enemy is using Bazookas? Of course not.

Learn how to decode HTF closures if you want to win the battle and eventually the war.

Otherwise, your destiny will be the same as Napoleon’s one in Waterloo.

2. Validity of a Candle Close

This is something that many people often ask me so I will try to clarify it a little bit. There are multiple aspects to consider when we try to evaluate if a closure is solid giving us more hint and therefore adjusting our next move. Generally, I use one main rule. I always pay close attention to the relationship between the body and the wicks, as it tells me a lot about the market's strength and sentiment.

  • A strong closure, to me, is when the body of the candle makes up at least 2/3 of the entire candlestick's range. This means that the open and close prices are significantly far apart, and there is little rejection in the form of wicks. If I see a bullish candle with a strong close near its high, I know that "buyers" dominated throughout the session, showing confidence and momentum. On the other hand, if a bearish candle closes strongly near its low, it tells me that "sellers" had complete control with little pushback from buyers. This kind of closure is what I look for when confirming trends or breakouts.
  • A moderate closure, however, is slightly different. Here, the body of the candle is at least 1/2 of the total candlestick range, but the wicks are more noticeable. This tells me that while momentum is present, there was some resistance or rejection along the way. If I see a bullish candle that closes higher but has a visible upper wick, I recognize that although buyers were in control, there was some selling pressure towards the end. It doesn’t necessarily mean a reversal, but it does make me cautious. In a bearish scenario, if the candle closes lower but with a significant lower wick, I understand that while sellers were dominant, buyers did step in to some extent. In these cases, I usually wait for additional confirmation before making a decision, such as waiting for the next 1 or 2 days.

Then there are weak closures, which I tend to avoid when looking for strong trade setups.

  • A weak closure happens when the body of the candle is less than 1/3 of the total length, meaning the wicks take up most of the candlestick. This kind of price action screams indecision so I generally stay away and wait longer since both "factions" aren't in control and they're still "battling". This could mean consolidation, hesitation, or even a potential reversal if it forms at key levels. A breakout attempt with a weak closure isn’t something I trust because it suggests uncertainty rather than conviction. To avoid confusion, I also don't pay attention to the specific "names" of candlesticks (doji, hanging man, hammer etc) but exclusively to their bodies. The only exception I make is for the Marubozu, which plays a significant role considering the strong bullish/bearish pressure that contains and which often leaves behind substantial liquidity.

In this example on the right you can clearly see a fakeout, with the price spiking above the previous pivotal high, but ending up closing well below, meaning that the buying pressure was fully absorbed.

This situation has trapped late longers and FOMO buyers, providing the necessary liquidity for continuing the move to the downside later. In fact, we saw another attempt toward the 107K region creating a lower high and then, a few days later, the real bearish impulse.

That’s why candle closures are so important.

I also help myself by using Fib levels, like in this BCH example where the candle broke out above the previous swing high generating an HTF BOS and closing above with at least 1/2 of the body.

This was a real sign of strength and continuation to the upside.

When analyzing the market therefore, I always keep these closure types in mind:

  • If I’m looking for a strong breakout, I want a strong closure beyond the last high to confirm that price is likely to continue in that direction.
  • If I see moderate closures, I remain cautious and look for further confirmation.
  • If I see weak closures, I know the market is still undecided, and I avoid jumping into trades prematurely.
